chemicals.name: While peanuts do not contain a specific, inherent chemical poison in the same way that onions or chocolate do

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: The severity depends on the dose; high doses result in acute, severe liver damage and sudden death, while prolonged low-dose exposure causes reduced growth rates and liver enlargement
chemicals.description: in cats: Peanuts are highly susceptible to becoming damp and developing Aspergillus mold, which produces aflatoxins14. If an animal ingests moldy peanuts, it causes aflatoxicosis. This disrupts normal cellular function, leading to severe liver damage, ruptured blood vessels (internal bleeding), and jaundice15. chemicals.name: Aflatoxins (Mold Contamination)

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: Allergic reactions are not strictly dose-dependent but rely on the individual animal's specific immune sensitivity
chemicals.description: in cats: The specific proteins in peanuts can trigger a severe immune system overreaction. The sources note that peanuts can specifically cause a "deadly allergic reaction" in pets3. Any notes on concentration or potency: Allergic reactions are not strictly dose-dependent but rely on the individual animal's specific immune sensitivity
chemicals.name: Peanut Allergens

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: Xylitol is extremely potent; in dogs, doses as low as 100 mg/kg cause severe hypoglycemia, and doses over 500 mg/kg cause liver failure
chemicals.description: in cats: Many commercial peanut butters use xylitol as an artificial, sugar-free sweetener26. In pets, xylitol tricks the pancreas into releasing a massive amount of insulin, which causes a rapid, life-threatening drop in blood sugar (hypoglycemia) and potential acute liver failure78. While dogs are exquisitely sensitive to this, and clinical data on cats is less conclusive, veterinary toxicologis
chemicals.name: Xylitol (Found in Peanut Butter)

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: The sources note that large amounts of salt can be fatal to pets1516. Treats containing these ingredients should make up no more than 10% of a pet's daily caloric intake, though peanuts are advised against entirely
chemicals.description: in cats: Peanuts are naturally very high in oils and fats, and human peanut snacks are frequently seasoned with high amounts of salt4.... A cat's digestive system cannot easily process dense plant fats, which leads to severe indigestion, diarrhea, and the potential to trigger pancreatitis (a painful inflammation of the pancreas)1314. Furthermore, the heavy salt content increases the metabolic burd
chemicals.name: High Fats and Added Salt (Processed Peanuts)

description: Plain, unsalted peanuts are not toxic to cats, but they are an inappropriate snack for several reasons. Peanuts are very high in fat, which can trigger pancreatitis with regular feeding. They also carry a risk of aflatoxin contamination (a toxic mould byproduct) and offer no nutritional benefit to obligate carnivores.
emergencyNote: Because peanuts do not contain a specific inherent chemical poison
name: Peanuts
safetyNotes: Not acutely toxic: plain peanuts do not contain cat-specific toxins
Fat risk: very high fat content; regular feeding can cause pancreatitis (painful, potentially fatal pancreatic inflammation)
Aflatoxin risk: mouldy or improperly stored peanuts may contain aflatoxins, which cause liver damage
Salted, flavoured, or roasted peanuts: high sodium and artificial seasonings are harmful to cats
Peanut butter: most brands contain xylitol or excessive salt — check labels; a tiny taste of plain peanut butter is unlikely to cause harm

symptoms.name: While plain, raw peanuts do not contain a specific inherent chemical poison
Aflatoxicosis (From Moldy Peanuts)
symptoms.notes: Peanuts are susceptible to growing Aspergillus fungi, which produce toxic aflatoxins1. Ingestion of these moldy peanuts results in severe liver damage and the rupturing of blood vessels
symptoms.onset: Acute (shortly after a large dose) or chronic (developing progressively over time with low-dose expo

symptoms.name: Hypoglycemia and Liver Failure (From Xylitol in Peanut Butter)
symptoms.notes: Many peanut butters are sweetened with xylitol, an artificial sweetener that tricks the body into releasing a massive amount of insulin37. While clinical data suggests cats are more resistant to xylitol than dogs, it can still cause a rapid drop in blood sugar and potentially fatal liver damage, req
symptoms.onset: 15 to 30 minutes for hypoglycemia (or delayed up to 12 hours depending on the product);

symptoms.name: Sodium Ion Poisoning (From Salted Peanuts)
symptoms.notes: Human peanut snacks are frequently heavily seasoned with salt, which unnecessarily increases the metabolic burden on a cat's kidneys4. An overdose of sodium draws fluid out of the body's cells, resulting in severe dehydration, electrolyte derangement, and potentially fatal kidney failure or cerebral
symptoms.onset: Within minutes to hours of ingestion

symptoms.name: Indigestion and Pancreatitis (From High Fat Content)
symptoms.notes: Peanuts are rich in oils and have a hard physical texture that easily leads to indigestion and diarrhea in cats4. Furthermore, consuming high-fat foods can trigger pancreatitis, a painful and potentially life-threatening inflammation of the pancreas
symptoms.onset: Within a few hours (for standard indigestion) or progressively over days (for pancreatitis)

symptoms.name: Severe Allergic Reaction
symptoms.notes: Peanuts have been specifically noted as a food item capable of triggering a deadly allergic reaction in pets

treatments.name: Because peanuts do not contain a specific inherent chemical poison

treatments.description: of the procedure: If the ingestion is caught very early (within a couple of hours) and the cat has not yet developed symptoms, the veterinarian may administer a specific injection to induce vomiting (emesis) to empty the stomach and minimize absorption1. If the cat is already showing symptoms of low blood sugar, they will be admitted for intensive in-patient monitoring and administered IV fluids c
treatments.name: Rapid Hypoglycemia Management & Decontamination (If Xylitol-sweetened peanut butter was ingested)
treatments.notes: Pet owners must never attempt to induce vomiting in cats at home, as there are no safe methods to do so without professional supervision3. While studies suggest cats are much more resistant to xylitol than dogs, any ingestion of this artificial sweetener requires prompt veterinary care as a precauti

treatments.description: of the procedure: The veterinarian will measure the cat's serum sodium and electrolyte levels and administer specific IV fluids to help dilute the sodium in the blood. The goal is to slowly return the animal to a normal water and electrolyte balance over a period of 2 to 3 days6.... Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Controlled Sodium Correction (If heavily salted peanuts were ingested)
treatments.notes: It is critical that the veterinarian lowers the sodium concentration slowly. If fluids are given too rapidly, it increases the osmotic gradient between the blood and the brain, drawing water into the brain cells and causing severe, potentially fatal cerebral edema (brain swelling)

treatments.description: of the procedure: If the cat experiences a severe immune overreaction, the vet will provide immediate life-saving stabilization (such as airway support, oxygen, and emergency medications) to counteract the systemic allergic response. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Emergency Anaphylaxis Response (If an allergic reaction occurs)
treatments.notes: Peanuts have been specifically identified as a food capable of triggering a "deadly allergic reaction" in pets

treatments.description: of the procedure: If the high fat in the peanuts triggers pancreatitis, the cat will be hospitalized and given IV fluids to correct dehydration and electrolyte abnormalities10. The vet will administer anti-nausea medications (such as maropitant) and prescribe pain relief (such as the opioid buprenorphine)10. To ensure the cat gets vital nutrients, the vet may use appetite stimulants (like mirtazap
treatments.name: Intensive Medical Management for Pancreatitis (If triggered by high fat content)
treatments.notes: Early nutritional support is critical in feline pancreatitis to prevent prolonged anorexia, which can quickly lead to a secondary, life-threatening liver condition known as hepatic lipidosis (fatty liver)
